name: Hydra (Configuration Framework for ML/Research)

performance.notes: Config composition at startup; negligible runtime overhead

agent_readiness.notes: Hierarchical config composition for ML experiments. @hydra.main(config_path='conf', config_name='config'). def main(cfg: DictConfig): print(cfg.db.host). conf/config.yaml: db: host: localhost port: 5432. conf/db/mysql.yaml for db group. Override on CLI: python main.py db=mysql training.lr=0.001 +new_key=value ~remove_key=. Sweep: python main.py --multirun db=mysql,postgres lr=0.001,0.01. Structured configs: @dataclass class DBConfig: host: str; port: int. OmegaConf integration. Config groups for modular configs. Instantiate objects: hydra.utils.instantiate(cfg.model). Logging config automatic. Working dir changes per run. Plugins: Optuna sweeper, Ray launcher.

gotchas: Changes working directory by default — can surprise with relative paths
Steep learning curve for config groups and composition
Not suitable for web services — designed for batch ML experiments
Complex configs can be hard to debug
best_when: ML experiment configuration with parameter sweeps
Research codebases needing modular config composition
When Optuna or Ray integration for hyperparameter search is needed
avoid_when: Web services or APIs (use pydantic-settings or dynaconf)
Simple scripts not needing hierarchical config
Teams unfamiliar with YAML config patterns
summary: Hydra is a hierarchical config framework for ML experiments. @hydra.main(config_path='conf', config_name='config'). YAML config files with groups. CLI overrides: python main.py db=mysql lr=0.001. Sweeps: --multirun db=mysql,postgres. hydra.utils.instantiate(). OmegaConf-backed. MIT by Meta. Changes working dir by default. Steep learning curve. Best for ML experiment grid search and modular config composition. Not for web services.
